paper_title: Geochemical Analysis and Data Interpretation of Shale Samples

methods.description: Process for preparing rock samples through grinding and analyzing them for total organic carbon (TOC), major elements, and trace elements using various analytical techniques.

steps.description: For each sample, two equal weight samples were weighed.
steps.searchable_summary: weigh rock samples
outputs: two equal weight rock sample portions

steps.description: ground into about 80 mesh and < 200 mesh powders, respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: grind rock samples to specified mesh sizes

steps.description: the 80 mesh powder was sent to the Analytical Experiment Center of the Exploration and Development Research Institute of Southwest Oil and Gas Field Company of Chengdu City, Sichuan Province, where the total organic carbon content (TOC) was measured using a LECO CS230 carbon sulfur analyzer.
steps.searchable_summary: send 80 mesh powder for TOC analysis

steps.description: the total organic carbon content (TOC) was measured using a LECO CS230 carbon sulfur analyzer.
steps.searchable_summary: measure total organic carbon (TOC) using LECO CS230

steps.description: The powder samples that were ground to < 200 mesh were sent to ALS Minerals-ALS Chemex, Guangzhou, Guangdong Province for major and trace elements testing.
steps.searchable_summary: send < 200 mesh powder for major and trace element analysis

steps.description: Major element (Si, Al, Ca, etc.) analysis involved burning the sample in a muffle oven at 1000 • C for 3-4 h.
steps.searchable_summary: burn sample in muffle oven for major element analysis preparation

steps.description: After the crucible was cooled, the ashed sample was weighed
steps.searchable_summary: cool and weigh ashed sample

steps.description: the loss on combustion was calculated for correction.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate loss on combustion

steps.description: About half of the ashed sample was mixed with 8-fold lithium tetraborate (Li 2 B 4 O 7 )
steps.searchable_summary: mix ashed sample with lithium tetraborate
outputs: mixture of ashed sample and flux

steps.description: fused into glass beads
steps.searchable_summary: fuse mixture into glass beads
inputs: mixture of ashed sample and flux

steps.description: analyzed using a PANalytical PW2424 x-ray fluorescence (XRF).
steps.searchable_summary: analyze glass beads for major elements using XRF

steps.description: Measurements of trace elements (Mo, U, V, etc.) require two samples. One sample was taken
steps.searchable_summary: take one < 200 mesh sample for trace element digestion

steps.description: digested with perchloric acid, nitric acid, and hydrofluoric acid.
steps.searchable_summary: digest sample with mixed acids

steps.description: After evaporation, dilute hydrochloric acid was added to dissolve to a constant volume,
steps.searchable_summary: evaporate digested solution and redissolve with dilute HCl to constant volume
outputs: prepared solution for ICP analysis (digestion method)

steps.description: an Agilent VISTA plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-AES) and an Agilent 7900 plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) were used for the analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze solution using ICP-AES and ICP-MS for trace elements
inputs: prepared solution for ICP analysis (digestion method)

steps.description: LiBO 2 /Li 2 B 4 O 7 solvent was added to another sample and melted at 1025 • C.
steps.searchable_summary: add flux to another sample and melt

steps.description: After cooling and diluting to a constant volume with dilute hydrochloric acid,
steps.searchable_summary: cool melted sample and dilute with dilute HCl to constant volume
outputs: prepared solution for ICP analysis (melting method)

steps.description: it was analyzed with an Agilent 7900 Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS).
steps.searchable_summary: analyze solution using ICP-MS for trace elements
inputs: prepared solution for ICP analysis (melting method)

steps.description: According to the results of the two measurements, the concentration of trace elements was obtained,
steps.searchable_summary: obtain trace element concentration from results of two measurement methods

methods.description: Method to calculate the enrichment factor (EF) for trace elements normalized to Aluminum, relative to average shale composition. This is used to distinguish autogenous components from terrigenous clastic components and assess enrichment or consumption relative to average shale.

steps.description: The trace element concentration is given in the form of an enrichment factor (EF), obtained by comparing the normalized concentration of aluminum with that of the average shale (AS) [28], and it is calculated as follows: where X and Al represent the weight concentrations of the elements X and Al, respectively, and average shale represents the composition of the Paleozoic to Cenozoic shale [28].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate trace element enrichment factor (EF) normalized to aluminum relative to average shale
inputs: weight concentration of element X in sample
weight concentration of Al in sample
weight concentration of element X in average shale
weight concentration of Al in average shale

methods.description: Method to calculate the concentration of biogenic barium (Ba_bio) in rock samples. This value is used as a proxy for paleoproductivity.

steps.description: The Ba concentration required for vital activities (Ba bio ) was calculated by Murray and Leinen [29]. where Ba tot and Al tot are the measured volume concentrations; and Ba/Al alu represents the Ba/Al ratio of aluminosilicate debris in the surface rock, which can represent the terrigenous influx of the sediments. The Ba/Al ratio of the aluminosilicate component in surface rock is between 0.005 and 0.01 [30], and the intermediate value, 0.0075, is used to calculate the Ba bio content [31].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate biogenic barium (Ba_bio) concentration
inputs: measured volume concentration of total Barium (Ba_tot)
measured volume concentration of total Aluminum (Al_tot)
Ba/Al ratio of aluminosilicate debris (Ba/Al_alu, typically 0.0075)

methods.description: Method to calculate the concentration of excess silica (biogenic silica) in rock samples. This value is used as a proxy for paleoproductivity.

steps.description: Through previous studies, it has been proven that, compared with the average shale, excess silica in the shale of the study area is biogenetic [16]. Therefore, its value can reflect the strength of productivity. The calculation formula is as follows [32]: where Si and Al represent the weight concentrations of the elements Si and Al, respectively; and the (Si/Al) background is calculated by shale from the Paleozoic to Cenozoic, with an average of 3.11 [28].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate excess silica (biogenic silica) concentration
inputs: weight concentration of Silicon (Si) in sample
weight concentration of Aluminum (Al) in sample
background Si/Al ratio for average shale (3.11)
